questions.question: A traditional organization has separate Dev and Ops teams. Developers are rewarded for shipping new features quickly, while operations are rewarded for keeping the system stable. What is the most direct consequence of this incentive structure?
options: Developers write higher-quality code because operations reviews every release before deployment.
A built-in conflict of interest emerges — speed and stability pull against each other, slowing releases and letting errors reach users.
Operations becomes the bottleneck only when release frequency exceeds once per week.
The two teams eventually merge because automation eliminates the need for separate roles.
questions.explanation: Opposing incentives — ship fast vs. stay stable — are the structural root cause of the Dev/Ops wall. Developers throw code 'over the wall'; operations resists deploying it because every change is a risk. This friction slows releases and allows errors to slip through. Option A is incorrect because operations reviewing every release is part of the problem (slow, bureaucratic hand-offs), not a quality benefit. Option C is wrong — the conflict is constant regardless of release cadence. Option D is wishful thinking; automation is only one element of DevOps and does not by itself cause teams to merge.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes what DevOps fundamentally is?
options: A specific set of tools — such as Jenkins, Docker, and Kubernetes — that automates the build-and-deploy chain.
A job title for engineers who manage both code deployments and server infrastructure.
A combination of cultural philosophies, practices, and tools, where culture comes first and tools support it.
A project management methodology similar to Scrum that replaces stand-ups with automated status dashboards.
questions.explanation: The canonical definition of DevOps emphasizes culture, practices, and tools — in that order. You can install every automation tool available and still fail at DevOps if the two teams do not communicate and share responsibility. Option A reduces DevOps to tooling, which is the most common misconception. Option B describes a role (sometimes called a DevOps engineer) but confuses the role with the movement itself. Option D conflates DevOps with Agile project management; while they are complementary, DevOps is specifically about breaking the delivery barrier between development and operations.

questions.question: In the CALMS framework, the 'A' stands for Automation. Which statement most accurately captures how CALMS treats automation?
options: Automation is the most important pillar; once the CI/CD pipeline is in place, the other pillars follow naturally.
Automation means eliminating repetitive manual work, but people and process must come first — automating a broken process only makes it break faster.
Automation applies only to testing; deployment must stay manual so that humans can catch last-minute errors.
Automation and Culture are interchangeable pillars; improving one automatically improves the other.
questions.explanation: CALMS treats automation as an enabler, not a starting point. The principle is explicit: 'automate the right things; a tool on a broken process just breaks faster.' You must fix the process and the culture first, then automate to make the improved process fast and consistent. Option A overstates automation's primacy — four of the five CALMS pillars are about people and process, not technology. Option C is wrong; CALMS advocates automating testing, building, and deploying. Option D conflates two distinct pillars; culture (breaking silos and sharing responsibility) and automation (eliminating manual work) address different problems.

questions.question: Your team tracks deployment frequency and lead time (time from commit to production). Which CALMS pillar are they practicing, and why does it matter for DevOps?
options: Lean — because tracking metrics reduces work-in-progress and keeps batch sizes small.
Sharing — because publishing metrics openly builds trust and tightens feedback loops across the organization.
Measurement — because data-driven decisions replace opinions, and you cannot improve what you do not measure.
Culture — because choosing which metrics to track reflects the team's shared values and psychological safety.
questions.explanation: Tracking deployment frequency, lead time, change failure rate, and mean time to recovery is the Measurement pillar of CALMS. The principle is straightforward: decisions should come from data, not gut feeling, and improvement requires a baseline to measure against. Option A is wrong — Lean is about reducing batch sizes and limiting work-in-progress in the delivery flow, not tracking metrics. Option B is wrong — Sharing is about spreading knowledge and feedback openly, not metric collection itself. Option D has a kernel of truth (culture influences what you measure) but the act of tracking operational metrics is unambiguously Measurement.
